(Italian: [[ˈtaːranto]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Taranto.ogg "File:It-Taranto.ogg"); [Tarantino](/wiki/Tarantino_dialect "Tarantino dialect"): *Tarde*),[[a]](#cite_note-5) historically also called **Tarent** in English, is a coastal city in [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia"), [Southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"). It is the capital of the [province of Taranto](/wiki/Province_of_Taranto "Province of Taranto"), serving as an important commercial port as well as the main Italian naval base.[[5]](#cite_note-6) With a population of 185,909 as of 2025, Taranto is the [second-largest](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_of_Apulia "List of municipalities of Apulia") city in Apulia.[[6]](#cite_note-population-7)

(Τάρᾱς, [gen.](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") Τάραντος *Tárantos*) after the mythical hero [Taras](/wiki/Taras_(mythology) "Taras (mythology)"), while the [Romans](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), who connected the city to Rome with an extension of the [Appian Way](/wiki/Appian_Way "Appian Way"), called it **Tarentum**.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

**Taranto** (Italian: [[ˈtaːranto]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Taranto.ogg "File:It-Taranto.ogg"); [Tarantino](/wiki/Tarantino_dialect "Tarantino dialect"): *Tarde*),[[a]](#cite_note-5) historically also called **Tarent** in English, is a coastal city in [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia"), [Southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"). It is the capital of the [province of Taranto](/wiki/Province_of_Taranto "Province of Taranto"), serving as an important commercial port as well as the main Italian naval base.[[5]](#cite_note-6) With a population of 185,909 as of 2025, Taranto is the [second-largest](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_of_Apulia "List of municipalities of Apulia") city in Apulia.[[6]](#cite_note-population-7)

Founded by Spartans in the 8th century BC during the period of [Greek colonisation](/wiki/Greek_colonisation "Greek colonisation"), Taranto was among the most important *[poleis](/wiki/Polis "Polis")* in [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia"), becoming a cultural, economic and military power that gave birth to philosophers, strategists, writers and athletes such as [Archytas](/wiki/Archytas "Archytas"), [Aristoxenus](/wiki/Aristoxenus "Aristoxenus"), [Livius Andronicus](/wiki/Livius_Andronicus "Livius Andronicus"), [Heracleides](/wiki/Heracleides_of_Tarentum "Heracleides of Tarentum"), [Iccus](/wiki/Iccus_of_Taranto "Iccus of Taranto"), [Cleinias](/wiki/Cleinias_of_Tarentum "Cleinias of Tarentum"), [Leonidas](/wiki/Leonidas_of_Tarentum "Leonidas of Tarentum"), [Lysis](/wiki/Lysis_of_Taras "Lysis of Taras") and [Sosibius](/wiki/Sosibius_of_Tarentum "Sosibius of Tarentum"). By 500 BC, the city was among the largest in the world, with a population estimated up to 300,000 people.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9) The seven-year rule of [Archytas](/wiki/Archytas "Archytas") marked the apex of its development and recognition of its [hegemony](/wiki/Hegemony "Hegemony") over other [Greek colonies](/wiki/Greek_colonisation "Greek colonisation") of southern Italy.

During the [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") period, it became the capital of the [Principality of Taranto](/wiki/Principality_of_Taranto "Principality of Taranto"), which covered almost all of the heel of [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia").

Taranto is now the third-largest continental city in southern Italy (south of Rome, roughly the southern half of the Italian peninsula), with well-developed steel and iron foundries, oil refineries, chemical works, naval shipyards and food-processing factories. Taranto will host the [2026 Mediterranean Games](/wiki/2026_Mediterranean_Games "2026 Mediterranean Games").

The islets of *[S. Pietro](/wiki/Cheradi_Islands "Cheradi Islands")* and *[S. Paolo](/wiki/San_Paolo_Island "San Paolo Island")* (St. Peter and St. Paul), collectively known as the [Cheradi Islands](/wiki/Cheradi_Islands "Cheradi Islands"), protect the bay, called *Mar Grande* (*Big Sea*), where the commercial port is located.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Taranto is known for the large population of [dolphins](/wiki/Dolphin "Dolphin") and other [cetaceans](/wiki/Cetacean "Cetacean") living near these islands. Another bay, called *Mar Piccolo* (*Little Sea*), is formed by the peninsula of the old city and is a popular location for [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The [Greek](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") colonists from [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta") called the city **Taras** (Τάρᾱς, [gen.](/wiki/Genitive_case "Genitive case") Τάραντος *Tárantos*) after the mythical hero [Taras](/wiki/Taras_(mythology) "Taras (mythology)"), while the [Romans](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), who connected the city to Rome with an extension of the [Appian Way](/wiki/Appian_Way "Appian Way"), called it **Tarentum**.[[9]](#cite_note-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main articles: [History of Taranto](/wiki/History_of_Taranto "History of Taranto") and [Timeline of Taranto](/wiki/Timeline_of_Taranto "Timeline of Taranto")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Temple_of_Poseidon_%28Taranto%29.jpg/250px-Temple_of_Poseidon_%28Taranto%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Temple_of_Poseidon_(Taranto).jpg)

[Doric columns](/wiki/Doric_columns "Doric columns") from the Temple of Poseidon in Taranto, legacy of its Greek origins.

Taranto was founded in 706 BC by [Dorian Greek](/wiki/Dorians "Dorians") immigrants hailing from [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta"). Its origin is peculiar: the founders were [Partheniae](/wiki/Partheniae "Partheniae") ("sons of virgins"), sons of unmarried Spartan women and *[Perioeci](/wiki/Perioeci "Perioeci")* (free men, but not citizens of Sparta); these out-of-wedlock unions were permitted extraordinarily by the Spartans to increase the prospective number of soldiers (only the citizens of Sparta could become soldiers) during the bloody [Messenian Wars](/wiki/Messenian_Wars "Messenian Wars"), but later they were retroactively nullified, and the sons were then obliged to leave Greece forever. [Phalanthus](/wiki/Phalanthus_of_Tarentum "Phalanthus of Tarentum"), the Parthenian leader and founder ([Oikistes](/wiki/Oikistes "Oikistes")), went to [Delphi](/wiki/Delphi "Delphi") to consult the [oracle](/wiki/Sibyl "Sibyl"): the puzzling answer designated the harbour of Taranto as the new home of the exiles. The Partheniae arrived in Apulia, and founded the city, naming it *Taras* after the son of the Greek sea god, [Poseidon](/wiki/Poseidon "Poseidon"), and of a local [nymph](/wiki/Nymph "Nymph"),[[10]](#cite_note-11) Satyrion.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] According to other sources, [Heracles](/wiki/Heracles "Heracles") founded the city. Another tradition indicates Taras as the founder of the city; the symbol of the Greek city (as well as of the modern city) depicts the legend of Taras being saved from a shipwreck by riding a dolphin that was sent to him by Poseidon. Taranto increased its power, becoming a commercial power and a sovereign city of [Magna Graecia](/wiki/Magna_Graecia "Magna Graecia").[[11]](#cite_note-italythisway1-12)
Politically and militarily, [Archytas](/wiki/Archytas "Archytas") appeared to have been the dominant figure in Tarentum in the first half of the 4th century, somewhat comparable to [Pericles](/wiki/Pericles "Pericles") in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") a half-century earlier.[[12]](#cite_note-13) The Tarentines elected him *[strategos](/wiki/Strategos "Strategos")* ("general") seven years in a row, a step that required them to violate their own rule against successive appointments. Archytas was allegedly undefeated as a general in Tarentine campaigns against their southern Italian neighbors.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

In 303, [Sparta](/wiki/Sparta "Sparta") sent [Cleonymus](/wiki/Cleonymus_of_Sparta "Cleonymus of Sparta"), the brother of king [Areus I](/wiki/Areus_I "Areus I"), commanding mercenary armies with official support in order to help Tarentum against [Lucanians](/wiki/Lucanians "Lucanians") and the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic").[[14]](#cite_note-15) Tarentum's power and independence came to an end as the Romans expanded throughout Italy. Taranto fought against Rome for the control of Southern Italy: it was helped by [Pyrrhus](/wiki/Pyrrhus_of_Epirus "Pyrrhus of Epirus"), [Molossian](/wiki/Molossian "Molossian") king of Greek [Epirus](/wiki/Epirus_(ancient_state) "Epirus (ancient state)"),[[11]](#cite_note-italythisway1-12) who surprised Rome with the use of [war elephants](/wiki/War_elephant "War elephant") in [battle of Heraclea](/wiki/Battle_of_Heraclea "Battle of Heraclea"), a thing never seen before by the Romans. After the Pyrrhic victory at the [battle of Asculum](/wiki/Battle_of_Asculum "Battle of Asculum") they lost the battle of Beneventum in 275 BC. Tarentum surrendered to Rome after the death of Pyrrhus in Peloponnese in 272 BC. This subsequently cut off Taranto from the centre of Mediterranean trade, by connecting the [Via Appia](/wiki/Via_Appia "Via Appia") directly to the port of Brundisium ([Brindisi](/wiki/Brindisi "Brindisi")).

### Ancient art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ancient art")]

See also: [Apulian vase painting](/wiki/Apulian_vase_painting "Apulian vase painting") and [Greek coinage of Italy and Sicily](/wiki/Greek_coinage_of_Italy_and_Sicily "Greek coinage of Italy and Sicily")

Like many Greek city states, Taras issued its own coins in the 5th and 4th centuries BC. The denomination was a Nomos, a die-cast silver coin whose weight, size and purity were controlled by the state. The highly artistic coins presented the symbol of the city, Taras being saved by a dolphin, with the reverse side showing the likeness of a [hippocamp](/wiki/Hippocamp "Hippocamp"), a horse-fish amalgam which is depicted in mythology as the beast that drew Poseidon's chariot.

Taras was also the centre of a thriving decorated [Greek pottery](/wiki/Greek_pottery "Greek pottery") industry during the 4th century BC. Most of the [South Italian](/wiki/South_Italian "South Italian") Greek vessels known as [Basilican](/wiki/Basilican "Basilican") ware were made in different workshops in the city.

Unfortunately, none of the names of the artists have survived, so modern scholars have been obliged to give the recognizable artistic hands and workshops nicknames based on the subject matter of their works, museums which possess the works, or individuals who have distinguished the works from others. Some of the most famous of the Apulian vase painters at Taras are now called: the [Iliupersis Painter](/w/index.php?title=Iliupersis_Painter&action=edit&redlink=1 "Iliupersis Painter (page does not exist)"), the [Lycurgus Painter](/w/index.php?title=Lycurgus_Painter&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lycurgus Painter (page does not exist)"), the [Gioia del Colle Painter](/w/index.php?title=Gioia_del_Colle_Painter&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gioia del Colle Painter (page does not exist)"), the [Darius Painter](/wiki/Darius_Painter "Darius Painter"), the [Underworld Painter](/wiki/Underworld_Painter "Underworld Painter"), and the [White Sakkos Painter](/w/index.php?title=White_Sakkos_Painter&action=edit&redlink=1 "White Sakkos Painter (page does not exist)"), among others.

The wares produced by these workshops were usually large elaborate vessels intended for mortuary use. The forms produced included [volute kraters](/wiki/Volute_krater "Volute krater"), [loutrophoroi](/wiki/Loutrophoros "Loutrophoros"), [paterai](/wiki/Patera "Patera"), [oinochoai](/wiki/Oinochoe "Oinochoe"), [lekythoi](/wiki/Lekythos "Lekythos"), [fish plates](/wiki/Fish_plate "Fish plate"), etc. The decoration of these vessels was [red figure](/wiki/Red_figure "Red figure") (with figures reserved in red clay fabric, while the background was covered in a black gloss), with overpainting ([sovradipinto](/w/index.php?title=Sovradipinto&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sovradipinto (page does not exist)")) in white, pink, yellow, and maroon slips.

Often the style of the drawings is florid and frilly, as was already the fashion in 4th-century Athens. Distinctive South Italian features also begin to appear. Many figures are shown seated on rocks. Floral motifs become very ornate, including spiraling vines and leaves, [roses](/wiki/Rose "Rose"), [lilies](/wiki/Lily "Lily"), [poppies](/wiki/Poppy "Poppy"), sprays of [laurel](/wiki/Lauraceae "Lauraceae"), [acanthus leaves](/wiki/Acanthus_(plant) "Acanthus (plant)"). Often the subject matter consists of naiskos scenes (scenes showing the statue of a deceased person in a naos, a miniature temple or shrine). Most often the naiskos scene occupies one side of the vase, while a mythological scene occupies the other. Images depicting many of the Greek myths are only known from South Italian vases, since Athenian ones seem to have had more limited repertoires of depiction.

### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: World War II")]

The [Battle of Taranto](/wiki/Battle_of_Taranto "Battle of Taranto") took place on the night of 11–12 November 1940 during the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") between British naval forces, under Admiral [Andrew Cunningham](/wiki/Andrew_Cunningham,_1st_Viscount_Cunningham_of_Hyndhope "Andrew Cunningham, 1st Viscount Cunningham of Hyndhope"),[[15]](#cite_note-16) and Italian naval forces, under Admiral [Inigo Campioni](/wiki/Inigo_Campioni "Inigo Campioni").[[16]](#cite_note-17) The [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") launched the first all-aircraft ship-to-ship naval attack in history, employing 21 [Fairey Swordfish](/wiki/Fairey_Swordfish "Fairey Swordfish") biplane [torpedo bombers](/wiki/Torpedo_bomber "Torpedo bomber") from the [aircraft carrier](/wiki/Aircraft_carrier "Aircraft carrier") [HMS *Illustrious*](/wiki/HMS_Illustrious_(87) "HMS Illustrious (87)") in the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea").[[17]](#cite_note-18) The attack struck the battle fleet of the *[Regia Marina](/wiki/Regia_Marina "Regia Marina")* at anchor in the harbour of Taranto, using [aerial torpedoes](/wiki/Aerial_torpedo "Aerial torpedo") despite the shallowness of the water.[[18]](#cite_note-navywings-19)

The Taranto Prize, defined as the "Biennial of the South", was a biennial cultural event that took place between 1947 and 1951.
It was born on the initiative of thirty-year-old veterans who, returning from the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), gathered in the «Cultural Club (Circolo della cultura)» and the newspaper 'Voce del Popolo'. The coordinator, Antonio Rizzo, was a physicist who graduated with Enrico Fermi. He intended to promote a new cultural impulse of a pacifist nature for the city.
The event was structured into two sections: literature and painting. Several artists of international calibre, such as [Pier Paolo Pasolini](/wiki/Pier_Paolo_Pasolini "Pier Paolo Pasolini"), [Carlo Emilio Gadda](/wiki/Carlo_Emilio_Gadda "Carlo Emilio Gadda"), and [Giorgio de Chirico](/wiki/Giorgio_de_Chirico "Giorgio de Chirico"), participated.
The theme of the competition was the sea.

### 2006 municipal bankruptcy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2006 municipal bankruptcy")]

The Municipality of Taranto was declared bankrupt effective 31 December 2005, having accrued liabilities of €357 million.[[19]](#cite_note-20) This was one of the biggest financial crises which has ever hit a municipality.

The bankruptcy declaration was made on 18 October 2006 by the receiver Tommaso Blonda. He was appointed following the resignation of the mayor, Rossana Di Bello, on account of her sixteen-month prison sentence for abuse of office and forgery of documents relating to investigations into the contract for the management of the city incinerator, awarded to Termomeccanica.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Taranto_%26_harbor_from_ISS_2017.JPG/500px-Taranto_%26_harbor_from_ISS_2017.JPG)](/wiki/File:Taranto_%26_harbor_from_ISS_2017.JPG)

Taranto and harbor from [ISS](/wiki/ISS "ISS"), 2017

Taranto faces the [Ionian Sea](/wiki/Ionian_Sea "Ionian Sea"). It is 14.5 m (48 ft) above [sea level](/wiki/Sea_level "Sea level"). It was built on a plain running north/north-west–southeast, and surrounded by the [Murgia](/wiki/Altopiano_delle_Murge "Altopiano delle Murge") plateau from the north-west to the east. Its territory extends for 209.64 km2 (80.94 mi2) and is mostly underwater. It is characterised by three natural [peninsulas](/wiki/Peninsula "Peninsula") and a man-made island, formed by digging a ditch during the construction of [Aragon Castle](/wiki/Castello_Aragonese_(Taranto) "Castello Aragonese (Taranto)"). The city is known as the "city of two seas" because it is washed by the Big Sea in the bay between Punta Rondinella to the northwest and Capo San Dante to the south, and by the vast reservoir of the Little Sea.

### Big Sea and Little Sea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Big Sea and Little Sea")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Mar_Piccolo_Taranto_panorama.jpg/250px-Mar_Piccolo_Taranto_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mar_Piccolo_Taranto_panorama.jpg)

The view of the Little Sea from the [Appian Way](/wiki/Appian_Way "Appian Way")

The Big Sea (or "Mare Grande") is frequently known as the *Big Sea bay* as that is where ships harbour. It is separated from the Little Sea (or "Mare Piccolo") by a cape which closes the gulf, leading to the artificial island. This island formed the heart of the original city and it is connected to the mainland by the [Ponte di Porta Napoli](/w/index.php?title=Ponte_di_Porta_Napoli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ponte di Porta Napoli (page does not exist)") and the [Ponte Girevole](/wiki/Ponte_Girevole "Ponte Girevole"). The Big Sea is separated from the Ionian Sea by the Capo San Vito, the Isole Cheradi of St Peter and St Paul, and the three islands of San Nicolicchio, which are completely incorporated by the [ILVA steelworks](/wiki/Ilva_(company) "Ilva (company)"). The latter form a little [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") which closes off the arc creating the natural Big Sea bay.

The Little Sea is considered to be a lagoon so it presents problems of water exchange.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It is virtually divided into two by the [Ponte Punta Penna Pizzone](/wiki/Ponte_Punta_Penna_Pizzone "Ponte Punta Penna Pizzone"), which joins the Punta Penna to the Punta Pizzone. The first of these forms a rough triangle, whose corners are the opening to the east and the Porta Napoli [channel](/wiki/Channel_(geography) "Channel (geography)") linking it to the Big Sea in the west.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The second half forms an [ellipse](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse") whose major axis measures almost 5 km (3 mi) from the south-west to the north-east. The [Galeso](/w/index.php?title=Galeso&action=edit&redlink=1 "Galeso (page does not exist)") river flows into the first half.

The two water bodies have slightly different winds and tides and their underwater springs have different salinities. These affect the currents on the surface and in the depths of the Big Sea and the two halves of the Little Sea. In the Big Sea and in the northern part of the Little Sea, there are some underwater springs called [citri](/wiki/Citro "Citro"),[[20]](#cite_note-21) which carry undrinkable freshwater together with salt water. This creates the ideal biological conditions for cultivating [Mediterranean mussels](/wiki/Mediterranean_mussel "Mediterranean mussel"), known locally as *cozze*.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

The climate of the city, recorded by the [weather station](/wiki/Weather_station "Weather station") situated near the [Grottaglie Military Airport](/wiki/Taranto-Grottaglie_Airport "Taranto-Grottaglie Airport"), is a [hot-summer Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Hot-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Hot-summer Mediterranean climate"),[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23) typical of the Mediterranean with frequent continental features.

The spring is usually mild and rainy, but it is not uncommon to have sudden cold spells from the north and east, which often cause snowfall. Average annual precipitation is fairly low (even for southern Italy), measuring just 425 mm (16.7 in) per year.

The summer is hot and humid, with temperatures averaging 29 °C (84 °F).

On 28 November 2012 a large F3 [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado") hit the port of Taranto and damaged the [Taranto Steel Mill](/wiki/Ilva_(company) "Ilva (company)"); about 20 workers were injured, and another man was reported missing.[[23]](#cite_note-24)

### Dialect

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Dialect")]

Main article: [Tarantino dialect](/wiki/Tarantino_dialect "Tarantino dialect")

The city is the centre of the [Tarantino dialect](/wiki/Tarantino_dialect "Tarantino dialect") (*dialètte tarandíne*) of the [Neapolitan language](/wiki/Neapolitan_language "Neapolitan language"). As a result of the city's history, it is influenced by Greek, Vulgar Latin, French and many others.

## Environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Environment")]

In 1991 Taranto was declared a high environmental risk area by the [Ministry of Environment](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Environment_(Italy) "Ministry of the Environment (Italy)"). As a consequence of the pollutants discharged into the air by the factories in the area, most notably the ILVA steel plant, part of [Riva Group](/wiki/Riva_Group "Riva Group"). 7% of Taranto's pollution is produced by the public; 93% is produced by factories. In 2005, the [European Pollutant Emission Register](/wiki/European_Pollutant_Emission_Register "European Pollutant Emission Register") estimated [dioxin](/wiki/Dioxins_and_dioxin-like_compounds "Dioxins and dioxin-like compounds") emissions from the Taranto ILVA plant were responsible for 83% of Italy's total reported emissions.[[32]](#cite_note-European_Commission_parliamentary_questions-33) Every year the city is exposed to 2.7 t (2.7 long tons; 3.0 short tons) of [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide") and 57.7 t (56.8 long tons; 63.6 short tons) of [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 2014, the Italian National Institute of Emissions and their Sources,[[33]](#cite_note-34) stated that Taranto stands third in the world behind China's [Linfen](/wiki/Linfen "Linfen"), and [Copşa Mică](/wiki/Cop%C5%9Fa_Mic%C4%83 "Copşa Mică") in Romania, the most polluted cities in the world due to factories' emissions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In particular, the city produces ninety-two percent of Italy's [dioxin](/wiki/Polychlorinated_dibenzodioxins "Polychlorinated dibenzodioxins").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] This is 8.8 percent of the dioxin in Europe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Between 1995 and 2004, [leukaemias](/wiki/Leukaemia "Leukaemia"), [myelomas](/wiki/Myeloma "Myeloma") and [lymphomas](/wiki/Lymphoma "Lymphoma") increased by 30 to 40 percent. Dioxin accumulates over the years. Over 9 kilos[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] of dioxin have been discharged into the city's air by its factories. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Grazing is banned within 20 km (12 mi) of the ILVA plant.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

In 2013, the ILVA plant was placed under special administration when its owner, the Riva family, was accused of failing to prevent toxic emissions,[[35]](#cite_note-36) which caused at least 400 premature deaths.[[36]](#cite_note-37) Emissions of both [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide"), [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") and dioxin have decreased. Animal species have returned that had left, including [swallows](/wiki/Swallows "Swallows"), [cranes](/wiki/Crane_(bird) "Crane (bird)"), [dolphins](/wiki/Dolphins "Dolphins"), [seahorses](/wiki/Seahorses "Seahorses") and the [coral reef](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

## Main sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Main sights")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Palazzo_del_governo_-_Taranto.jpg/250px-Palazzo_del_governo_-_Taranto.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palazzo_del_governo_-_Taranto.jpg)

Palazzo del Governo facing the *Lungomare*, which was inaugurated in 1934 by Benito Mussolini

Taranto has a number of sites of historic value. Situated at the angle of the canal, Big Sea and *Piazza Castello*, the [Aragon Castle](/wiki/Castello_Aragonese_(Taranto) "Castello Aragonese (Taranto)") was built between 1486 and 1492 by orders of King [Ferdinand II of Aragon](/wiki/Ferdinand_II_of_Aragon "Ferdinand II of Aragon") to protect the city from the [Turks](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")' frequent raids.[[37]](#cite_note-puglia-38) The castle, which was designed by Italian painter and architect [Francesco di Giorgio Martini](/wiki/Francesco_di_Giorgio_Martini "Francesco di Giorgio Martini"), replaced a pre-existing 9th-century Byzantine fortress, which was deemed unfit for 15th-century warfare.[[37]](#cite_note-puglia-38)[[38]](#cite_note-palazzi-39) In 1707 it ceased to be used as a military fortress and was converted to a prison until under [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") it reverted to its original military function. To date it is the property of the Italian Navy and is open to the public. Twenty-first-century excavations revealed the castle's earlier Byzantine foundations which can be viewed.[[37]](#cite_note-puglia-38)

There are several Greek temple ruins - some from the 6th century BC - such as the remains of a [temple](/wiki/Temple_of_Poseidon_(Taranto) "Temple of Poseidon (Taranto)") dedicated to Poseidon, with its two surviving Doric columns still visible on Piazza Castello in the *Città Vecchia*.[[39]](#cite_note-taranto2004-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Taranto_-_Corso_Due_Mari.jpg/250px-Taranto_-_Corso_Due_Mari.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taranto_-_Corso_Due_Mari.jpg)

The *Corso Due Mari*, facing the Old town.

The [Promenade](/wiki/Promenade "Promenade") (*lungomare*), named after former Italian king [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III "Victor Emmanuel III"), overlooks the *Mar Grande*, the natural harbour and commercial port.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Concattedrale Gran Madre di Dio](/wiki/Concattedrale_Gran_Madre_di_Dio "Concattedrale Gran Madre di Dio"), designed by [Gio Ponti](/wiki/Gio_Ponti "Gio Ponti"), was built in 1967–1971 in reinforced concrete and is one of the most significant late works by the architect. In 2018 it is in poor condition and defaced by graffiti.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

In the modern districts, but above all in the central *Borgo Umbertino*, there are also the [Fountain of the *Rosa dei Venti*](/w/index.php?title=Fountain_of_the_Rosa_dei_Venti&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fountain of the Rosa dei Venti (page does not exist)"), [Monumento al Marinaio](/w/index.php?title=Monumento_al_Marinai&action=edit&redlink=1 "Monumento al Marinai (page does not exist)"), the [War Memorial](/w/index.php?title=War_Memorial_of_Taranto&action=edit&redlink=1 "War Memorial of Taranto (page does not exist)") and the [Navy Yard](/w/index.php?title=Navy_Yard_of_Taranto&action=edit&redlink=1 "Navy Yard of Taranto (page does not exist)"), another symbol of the city, some archeological sites such as the [Cripta del Redentore](/w/index.php?title=Cripta_del_Redentore&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cripta del Redentore (page does not exist)"), churches like *[Maria Santissima del Monte Carmelo](/w/index.php?title=Maria_Santissima_del_Monte_Carmelo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maria Santissima del Monte Carmelo (page does not exist)")*, [*San Pasquale*](/w/index.php?title=San_Pasquale,_Taranto&action=edit&redlink=1 "San Pasquale, Taranto (page does not exist)") and [*San Francesco di Paola*](/w/index.php?title=San_Francesco_di_Paola,_Taranto&action=edit&redlink=1 "San Francesco di Paola, Taranto (page does not exist)") and 18th- and 19th-century palaces such as [Palazzo Magnini](/w/index.php?title=Palazzo_Magnini&action=edit&redlink=1 "Palazzo Magnini (page does not exist)"), Palazzo delle Poste, [Palazzo del Governo](/w/index.php?title=Palazzo_del_Governo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Palazzo del Governo (page does not exist)"), [Palazzo degli Uffici](/w/index.php?title=Palazzo_degli_Uffici&action=edit&redlink=1 "Palazzo degli Uffici (page does not exist)") and [Palazzo Savino D'Amelio](/w/index.php?title=Palazzo_Savino_D%27Amelio&action=edit&redlink=1 "Palazzo Savino D'Amelio (page does not exist)").

On the outskirts and in the countryside there are several traditional ancient country houses called *masseria*, like [Masseria Capitignano](/w/index.php?title=Masseria_Capitignano&action=edit&redlink=1 "Masseria Capitignano (page does not exist)").

### Old city

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Old city")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Taranto_ViaCava_Copy_12-10-2015.jpg/250px-Taranto_ViaCava_Copy_12-10-2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taranto_ViaCava_Copy_12-10-2015.jpg)

Via Cava in Old City. The painted red arched door of the old, multi-storied palazzo is an example of the [street art](/wiki/Street_art "Street art") that since 2013 became a feature of Old City

The Old City or *Città Vecchia* is where the Greeks built their acropolis. Today it retains the same street layout of 967, when the Byzantines under Nicephorus Phocas rebuilt what the [Saracen](/wiki/Saracen "Saracen") troops led by the Slavic Sabir had razed to the ground in 927 AD.[[41]](#cite_note-oldtarantobari-42) There are four main arteries (Corso Vittorio II, Via Duomo, Via di Mezzo and Via Garibaldi) which run in a straight direction however the side streets were purposely built narrow and winding to impede the passage of an invading army.[[42]](#cite_note-brit-43)

Incorporating the [Aragon Castle](/wiki/Castello_Aragonese_(Taranto) "Castello Aragonese (Taranto)"), [Doric Columns](/wiki/Temple_of_Poseidon_(Taranto) "Temple of Poseidon (Taranto)"), City Hall, Clock Tower and Piazza Fontana, it is situated and entirely enclosed on the artificial island between the Big and Little Seas and is reached from the New City
by crossing the Ponte Girevole ([swing bridge](/wiki/Swing_bridge "Swing bridge")) from the south and the Ponte di Porta Napoli from the north. Almost rectangular in shape, it is divided into four *pittaggi* (quarters) that are delineated by the cross formed between Via di Mezzo and *postilier* Via Nuova. These are "Baglio" and "San Pietro" in the upper section which face the Big Sea; and "Turipenne" and "Ponte" in the lower part fronting the Little Sea.[[41]](#cite_note-oldtarantobari-42) The nobility, clergy and military personnel made their homes in Baglio and San Pietro, whilst the artisans and fishermen dwelled in Ponte and Turipenne. An Armenian community was present in the 10th and 11th centuries having arrived in Taranto as troops in the Byzantine Army. The *San't Andrea degli Armeni* church in Piazza Monteoliveto, located in the Baglio quarter, stands as testimony to the neighbourhood where the Armenians made their homes.[[43]](#cite_note-melograni-44)

In 1746 the entire population of Taranto resided in Old City. This resulted in the necessity of building additional stories on the narrow houses.[[44]](#cite_note-45) It is still inhabited with a number of people living in juxtaposition to the old palazzi. By 2013 the population of the Old City was just 1000[[41]](#cite_note-oldtarantobari-42) at a time when the wider city had more than 200,000 inhabitants.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Tarantomezzo.jpeg/250px-Tarantomezzo.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Tarantomezzo.jpeg)

Old derelict buildings in Via di Mezzo which forms the cross that divides the four pittagi

There are a number of 17th and 18th-century *palazzi* in Old City. For years, they served as the main residence of local aristocratic families and the clergy. These include Palazzo Calò, Palazzo Carducci-Artenisio (1650), Palazzo Galeota (1728), Palazzo Gallo (17th century), P PMalazzo Latagliata, Palazzo Lo Jucco (1793), Palazzo D'Aquino, Palazzo Delli Ponti, Palazzo Gennarini, Palazzo d'Ayala, Palazzo Visconti, Palazzo Galizia, Palazzo Ciura and Palazzo Pantaleo. The 17th century de Beaumont-Bonelli-Bellacicco palace houses the Spartan Museum of Taranto - Hypogeum Bellacicco which extends below street and sea level to the hypogeum that is a crossroads with other hypogeum of Old City which together form the system of subterranean Taranto.[[38]](#cite_note-palazzi-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Taranto_-_Duomo_di_San_Cataldo_-_11.jpg/250px-Taranto_-_Duomo_di_San_Cataldo_-_11.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taranto_-_Duomo_di_San_Cataldo_-_11.jpg)

Ceiling of the San Cataldo chapel inside [the cathedral](/wiki/Taranto_Cathedral "Taranto Cathedral").

Churches include the *[San Cataldo Cathedral](/wiki/Taranto_Cathedral "Taranto Cathedral")* (10th century) in Piazza Duomo, *[San Domenico Maggiore](/w/index.php?title=San_Domenico_Maggiore,_Taranto&action=edit&redlink=1 "San Domenico Maggiore, Taranto (page does not exist)")* (1302), Sant'Andrea degli Armeni (16th century), *[Sant'Agostino](/w/index.php?title=Sant%27Agostino,_Taranto&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sant'Agostino, Taranto (page does not exist)")* (1402), *[San Michele](/w/index.php?title=San_Michele,_Taranto&action=edit&redlink=1 "San Michele, Taranto (page does not exist)")* (1763), *[Sant'Anna](/w/index.php?title=Sant%27Anna_Church,_Taranto&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sant'Anna Church, Taranto (page does not exist)")*, the *[Madonna della Salute](/w/index.php?title=Madonna_della_Salute_Sanctuary&action=edit&redlink=1 "Madonna della Salute Sanctuary (page does not exist)")* sanctuary (1752), and *[San Giuseppe](/w/index.php?title=San_Giuseppe,_Taranto&action=edit&redlink=1 "San Giuseppe, Taranto (page does not exist)")* (16th century).[[38]](#cite_note-palazzi-39) Close to the San Agostino church, located near Pendio La Riccia, the buried remains of an ancient Greek temple were discovered.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Beginning in 1934 [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") embarked on a project of rejuvenation that involved the demolition of the working class Turipenne *pittaggio* along the Via Garibaldi and *Discesa Vasto* which contained the homes of local fishermen as well as the old Jewish quarter. The demolitions, which also razed the old medieval wall and three churches out of the four within the area, continued until the outbreak of World War II. Modern edifices and apartment blocks were erected to replace the demolished structures.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In addition to the many *palazzi*, Old City has myriad arched alleyways, *saliti*, vicoli and small streets, some of which are closed to traffic. Between 2013 and 2014 two Neapolitan urban artists Cyop and Kaf embarked on a project to decorate derelict buildings, walls and doors in the *piazzi* and vicoli with 120 representations of [street art](/wiki/Street_art "Street art"). It has since become a striking feature of Old City which is described as the abandoned district of Taranto.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

## Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

Taranto's cuisine is characterised by local products, especially vegetables and fish like [artichokes](/wiki/Artichoke "Artichoke"), [eggplants](/wiki/Eggplant "Eggplant"), [tomatoes](/wiki/Tomato "Tomato"), [olives](/wiki/Olive "Olive"), [onions](/wiki/Onion "Onion"), [shrimps](/wiki/Shrimp "Shrimp"), [octopus](/wiki/Octopus "Octopus"), [sardines](/wiki/Sardines_as_food "Sardines as food"), [squid](/wiki/Squid "Squid") and, above all, [mussels](#Mussels_of_Taranto). A very important role is also played by the [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil") and bread produced in the city and in all the villages of its province. Some [PDO](/wiki/Protected_designation_of_origin "Protected designation of origin"), [PGI](/wiki/Protected_Geographical_Indication "Protected Geographical Indication") and [PAT](/wiki/Prodotti_agroalimentari_tradizionali "Prodotti agroalimentari tradizionali") are made in the countryside of Taranto and in the villages around the city: among them we can find some extra-virgin olive oil like [Terre Tarentine PDO](/wiki/Terre_Tarentine "Terre Tarentine") and [Terra d'Otranto PDO](/wiki/Terra_d%27Otranto_(extra-virgin_olive_oil) "Terra d'Otranto (extra-virgin olive oil)"), fruits like [Uva di Puglia PGI](/wiki/Uva_di_Puglia_PGI "Uva di Puglia PGI") and [Clementine del Golfo di Taranto PGI](/wiki/Clementine#Varieties "Clementine"), vegetables like the [Barattiere PAT](/wiki/Carosello_(melon) "Carosello (melon)"), [Pomodorino di Manduria](/wiki/Pomodorino_di_Manduria "Pomodorino di Manduria"), types of cheese like [Burrata di Andria PGI](/wiki/Burrata "Burrata") and [Ricotta Forte PAT](/wiki/Ricotta_forte "Ricotta forte"), a type of bread called [Pane di Laterza PAT](/wiki/Pane_di_Laterza "Pane di Laterza") and the Capocollo di Martina Franca PAT, a type of *[capocollo](/wiki/Capocollo "Capocollo")*.
Other appreciated street foods are the [tarallini](/wiki/Tarallini "Tarallini"), the [panzerotti](/wiki/Panzerotti "Panzerotti"), the [pucce](/w/index.php?title=Pucce&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pucce (page does not exist)").

### Mussels of Taranto

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Mussels of Taranto")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Cozza_tarantina.jpg/250px-Cozza_tarantina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cozza_tarantina.jpg)

Tarantinian mussels cooked in a pan

A very important ingredient of the cuisine of Taranto is [mussels](/wiki/Mussel "Mussel"). They are grown in the Big Sea and, above all, in the Little Sea (see [above](#Big_Sea_and_Little_Sea)). They have been inserted in the list of Traditional Food Products by the Italian [Ministry of Agricultural, Food and Forestry Policies](/wiki/Ministry_of_Agricultural,_Food_and_Forestry_Policies_(Italy) "Ministry of Agricultural, Food and Forestry Policies (Italy)"). The peculiar flavour of Tarantine mussels is given by the special conditions of salinity of the Little Sea which is crossed by the *citri*, submarine freshwater springs which manage to oxygenate the water, helping the development of the [plankton](/wiki/Plankton "Plankton") and by the freshwater come from the Galeso river. The piles for the mussels were anciently made with wood from [Sila Mountains](/wiki/La_Sila "La Sila") in [Calabria](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria"). During the Ancient Greek and Roman times, several authors[[47]](#cite_note-48) described the richness and the goodness of the mussels of Taranto. After the tests about the pollution that is present in the first side of the Little Sea, the legal production of mussels has been moved to the second side. The tests and the classifications of the water are made by producers giving the possibility to certify the safety of the product.[[48]](#cite_note-49) Some of the most traditional dishes of Taranto are mussels *alla puppitegna* (with [garlic](/wiki/Garlic "Garlic"), extra-virgin olive oil and [parsley](/wiki/Parsley "Parsley")) or the *[impepata](/w/index.php?title=Impepata&action=edit&redlink=1 "Impepata (page does not exist)")* ("full of pepper" in Italian) or [spaghetti](/wiki/Spaghetti "Spaghetti") with mussels, or [Tubettini](/wiki/Tubettini "Tubettini") with mussels.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Ponte_Girevole_%28Taranto%29_opening_1.jpg/250px-Ponte_Girevole_%28Taranto%29_opening_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ponte_Girevole_(Taranto)_opening_1.jpg)

The two leaves of the 1887-built Ponte Girevole turning to open the waterway for a large ship

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Rail")]

[Taranto railway station](/wiki/Taranto_railway_station "Taranto railway station") connects the city with [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), [Bari](/wiki/Bari "Bari"), [Reggio di Calabria](/wiki/Reggio_di_Calabria "Reggio di Calabria") and [Brindisi](/wiki/Brindisi "Brindisi").

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Air")]

[Taranto-Grottaglie Airport](/wiki/Taranto-Grottaglie_Airport "Taranto-Grottaglie Airport") is located 16 km away from Taranto, but does not offer any regularly scheduled commercial services. The two closest airports that do offer regularly scheduled commercial services are in [Brindisi](/wiki/Brindisi_Airport "Brindisi Airport") and [Bari](/wiki/Bari_Karol_Wojty%C5%82a_Airport "Bari Karol Wojtyła Airport"), approximately 70 km and 90 km away, respectively.

### Other

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Taranto&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Other")]

The [Ponte Girevole](/wiki/Ponte_Girevole "Ponte Girevole") ([swing bridge](/wiki/Swing_bridge "Swing bridge")), built in 1887, runs across the navigable ship canal that joins *Mar Piccolo* (*Little Sea*) with *Mar Grande* (*Big Sea*) and stretches along 89.9 m (295 ft). When the bridge is open, the two ends of the city are disconnected.
